U9 / Novice.why: Spreading out on a rush forces the defender to make an impossible choice. A tight group lets one player cover all three.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rush attack: spread out. Make the defender choose.
opts: All three skate side-by-side close together.
All three skate single file straight at the goalie.
Spread out wide so the defender can't cover everyone.
Two go to the net and one wait at the blue line.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is loose in the corner and nobody is near it. What do you do?
opts: Go stand in front of the net.
Wait and see who gets it.
Stay put and call for it.
Skate hard toward the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: Loose pucks belong to whoever wants them most. Going hard after every loose puck is one of the most important habits you can build.
U9 / Novice.tip: Loose puck? Go get it. Every time. That's compete.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck in the o-zone. Three opponents are crowded around them. What should you do?
opts: Find an open space away from the crowd — near the net or at the blue line.
Stand still in your spot and wait for someone to pass you the puck there.
Skate to the bench and take a quick rest while your teammates keep playing.
Go join the crowd and chase the puck with your teammates near the boards.
U9 / Novice.why: Crowding around the puck carrier makes it harder for them, not easier. Finding open ice gives them a passing option and stretches the defense.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate has the puck and everyone's crowding them? Go find open ice. Give them an option.

U9 / Novice.sit: You've been skating hard for a long time and your legs are getting tired. What's the right move?
U9 / Novice.why: Changing lines keeps your team fresh. When there's a stoppage and you're tired, getting off the ice and letting a fresh teammate on helps your team.
U9 / Novice.tip: Tired during a stoppage? Get to the bench. Let a fresh teammate in.
opts: Keep playing — you don't want to come off.
Skate to the bench and change before you cost your team.
Stand still and wait for the whistle.
Skate slowly so you can stay on the ice longer.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate is in a battle for the puck, getting pushed around. You're nearby without the puck. How can you help?
opts: Skate toward your teammate — being close creates support and could help them make a play.
Skate to the other end of the rink and wait there for the puck to come down to you.
Watch — there's nothing you can do without the puck so just wait and let them handle it.
Call out to the referee and ask them to blow the whistle and stop the play.
U9 / Novice.why: Being close to a teammate who's in a battle gives them a passing option and moral support. Hockey is about playing together — be there for your teammates.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate in a battle? Get close and be their option. Support them.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is on a rush with more players than the other team. Where should you be on the ice?
opts: Skate as close to the puck carrier as you possibly can every time.
Spread across the ice so the defenders have to cover more space.
Skate behind the puck carrier the whole way and follow them.
Stop skating and watch the puck carrier handle it by themselves.
U9 / Novice.why: Spreading out on a rush forces defenders to cover more space, opening up passing lanes and shooting opportunities.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rush attack? Spread across the ice. Give the defenders more to think about.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're wide open in the o-zone. Your teammate has the puck but hasn't seen you. What do you do?
opts: Call your teammate's name to let them know you're open.
Skate toward them quickly to get the puck yourself and try to score it.
Wait patiently and hope they turn around and spot you all by themselves.
Stay quiet — yelling for the puck is rude to the other team's players.
U9 / Novice.why: Letting your teammate know you're open is the right play. A loud 'here!' gives them a target and might lead directly to a scoring chance.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open and your teammate can't see you? Call for the puck. Let them know.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck and a teammate is wide open beside you with no defender near them. What's the best play?
U9 / Novice.why: Hockey is a team game. Open teammate + blocked path = pass. This is the beginning of understanding that passing can be smarter than carrying.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open teammate + your path blocked = pass. That's real hockey thinking.
opts: Shoot the puck wide to get a faceoff.
Wait for your teammate to come closer.
Try to skate around all the defenders yourself.
Pass the puck to your open teammate.

U9 / Novice.sit: After a drill, your coach tells you something you did wrong and how to fix it. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: Feedback from coaches is how you get better. A coach who corrects you is investing in you. Listening, saying thank you, and trying the correction is exactly what the best players do.
U9 / Novice.tip: Coach gave you feedback? Say thanks and try it their way. That's how you improve.
opts: Get upset and skip the next drill.
Listen and try to do it better next time.
Ignore it — your coach is wrong.
Tell the coach you already knew that.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team has a 2-on-1. Your teammate has the puck, you don't. What should you do?
opts: Stop skating and watch your teammate handle the whole play all by themselves — they've got it from here.
Skate straight to the bench and take a break — your teammate can handle this 2-on-1 rush all by themselves.
Skate into open space — go to the side where the defender isn't. Give your teammate a pass option.
Skate straight toward your teammate and get right next to them so you can help them handle the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: On a 2-on-1, the player without the puck needs to get to the open side. If the defender takes your teammate, you're wide open for the pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1 without the puck? Get to the open side. Be the option.

U9 / Novice.sit: You accidentally passed it to the other team and they scored. Your teammates are looking at you. What now?
opts: Apologize a lot and then feel really terrible about it for the rest of the game.
Blame someone else on your team for the mistake you just made out there.
Stop trying to make any more passes to your teammates for the rest of the hockey game.
Put your head up and keep going — everyone makes mistakes. Focus on the next play.
U9 / Novice.why: Mistakes happen to every player in every game. The best response is to acknowledge it quickly and refocus on the next play. Staying in the moment instead of dwelling on a mistake is a real skill.
U9 / Novice.tip: Made a mistake? Head up. Next play. Everyone does it.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your coach is explaining a drill and you're not sure what to do. What's the best thing?
opts: Sit down on the ice until someone comes to help you.
Do whatever the kid next to you is doing every time.
Listen carefully, then ask what you didn't understand.
Just start skating and hope you guess the drill right.
U9 / Novice.why: Coaches give instructions to help you succeed. Listening well and asking questions is smart, not weak.
U9 / Novice.tip: Confused? Listen first, then ask. That's how you get better.

U9 / Novice.sit: Every time a puck is loose, you and your opponents are racing for it. What's the most important thing about who wins?
U9 / Novice.why: Making it a habit to race to every loose puck — not just the ones you think you'll win — is what separates compete players from passive ones. Even if you don't always win the race, the habit itself makes you better.
U9 / Novice.tip: Loose puck anywhere on the ice? Race to it. Make it a habit. Every single time.
opts: Who is the closest to start.
Who is the bigger player.
Who works harder and skates faster.
Who has the better stick.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your coach yells 'back!' What does that usually mean?
opts: Skate back toward your own goalie.
Back up to the bench and sit.
Lean backward on your skates.
Skate backward in circles.
U9 / Novice.why: 'Back!' means get back to your own end. Your team is defending and they need you home.
U9 / Novice.tip: 'Back!' = skate to your goalie.

U9 / Novice.sit: You just crossed your own blue line into the middle of the rink. Which zone are you in now?
opts: Offensive zone.
Neutral zone.
Penalty zone.
Defensive zone.
U9 / Novice.why: Crossing out of your own zone puts you in the neutral zone. Good awareness!
U9 / Novice.tip: Between the blues = neutral.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're down and out of position. The shooter has an open net. You can't get up in time. What do you do?
opts: Stay completely still on the ice and hope the shooter somehow misses the wide-open net that you've given them with the mistake.
Call for help from your defensemen so they can slide in front of the shooter and take away the shooting lane from where you're down.
Give up the goal and just see what happens next because there's really nothing else you can do from flat on the ice like this.
Use whatever you can reach — your stick, your glove, your skate — to get something in the way. Any part of you in the lane is better than nothing.
U9 / Novice.why: Desperation saves are real. Even if you're out of position, getting any part of your body or equipment into the shooting lane can change a goal into a save. Never stop until the whistle.
U9 / Novice.tip: Out of position? Reach for it anyway. Desperate saves happen. Never quit.

U9 / Novice.sit: You make a save but you can direct where the rebound goes. Where should you try to send it?
opts: Over the boards and out of play to stop the attack.
Into the corner where your team can retrieve it.
Anywhere far from the net and away from trouble.
Straight back into the slot where a teammate might be.
U9 / Novice.why: Directing rebounds to the corner keeps the puck away from the most dangerous area — the slot. Your team can retrieve it in the corner and start a breakout.
U9 / Novice.tip: Control your rebounds. Aim for the corners, not the middle.

U9 / Novice.sit: An opponent is screening you in front, blocking your view of a point shot. The shot is coming. What do you do?
opts: Move laterally to try to find a sightline, and be ready to react to tips and deflections.
Call for your teammates to clear the screener and wait for them to move the player away.
Skate hard into the screener to knock them out of the way before the shot gets released.
Give up on seeing the puck and just hope the shot hits some part of your equipment cleanly.
U9 / Novice.why: Screeners are a real problem but not impossible. Moving slightly helps you find the puck, and being ready for a deflection means you're not just frozen behind the screen.
U9 / Novice.tip: Screener in front? Move to find the puck. Expect a tip. React.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player is on a breakaway coming straight at you. They're moving fast. When should you commit to going down?
opts: Never go down at all — always stay up on your skates no matter what the shooter chooses to do.
As soon as they cross the blue line so you are ready for the shot before it gets released.
Wait until they've made their move — commit when you see what they're going to do, not before.
Right away as soon as they cross your blue line — drop into the butterfly immediately and wait.
U9 / Novice.why: The goalie who commits too early loses. A patient goalie who waits for the shooter's move before committing forces the shooter to beat them rather than just beat the commit.
U9 / Novice.tip: Breakaway: be patient. Make them show you something first. Then commit.

U9 / Novice.sit: Goalie makes a save and has the puck. Forecheckers are coming. Your winger breaks back. What should the goalie do?
opts: Pass to the winger breaking back — beat the forecheck.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far side winger.
Shoot the puck all the way down the ice to the far end.
Hold the puck under the glove and freeze it for a faceoff play.
U9 / Novice.why: A goalie with the puck can start the breakout before the forecheck resets. A crisp pass to the returning winger beats the pressure.
U9 / Novice.tip: Goalie has it and a winger is coming back? Start the breakout fast.

U9 / Novice.sit: You just won the puck in your zone. Their forecheckers are still deep. What do you do?
opts: Skate it out calmly — the forecheckers are out of position and you have time.
Pass it back to your goalie and let them freeze it for a faceoff.
Wait behind your own net for the forecheckers to get tired and regroup.
Shoot it down the ice to the far end as fast as you can.
U9 / Novice.why: When the other team's forecheckers are caught deep in your zone, they're out of position. That's your window to carry the puck out with speed.
U9 / Novice.tip: Forecheckers caught deep? Carry it out. You have time.

U9 / Novice.sit: You just turned the puck over in the o-zone. The other team is skating away. You're the nearest forward. First move?
opts: Go to the bench as fast as you can.
Stay in the offensive zone in case.
Skate slowly back and watch.
Immediately turn and skate back.
U9 / Novice.why: The instant the puck changes hands, your job changes. You go from attacker to backchecker immediately.
U9 / Novice.tip: Turnover = turn around. Don't wait. Go now.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your D partner went behind the net for the puck. A forward is crashing the net uncovered. What do you do?
opts: Wait for your D partner to come back out from behind the net.
Call out the open player to your teammates and cover them yourself.
Stand in front of your own net and let the forward battle your goalie.
Leave them alone — the goalie has a good view and will make the save.
U9 / Novice.why: When your D partner leaves to get the puck, you need to cover the open attacker. Calling it out also helps your team organize.
U9 / Novice.tip: D partner left? You cover the open player. Call it out loud.

U9 / Novice.sit: Turnover in the NZ. The other team has a 2-on-2 toward your net. You're one of the two D. Your priority?
opts: Both defenders collapse down into the crease and pack the net to block every shot attempt.
Skate straight back to the goal line and let the goalie handle the 2-on-2 rush on their own.
Take your side — one defender takes the carrier, the other takes the second attacker — and protect the middle.
Both defenders chase the puck carrier hard and try to strip the puck before they get a shot off.
U9 / Novice.why: A 2-on-2 defense requires each defender to take a side. The most dangerous play is a middle pass — stay in the lanes and make them go wide.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-2 against you: each take a side. Protect the middle.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your PK clears the puck deep into their zone. You're first over their blue line. What do you do?
opts: Slow down because it's probably just going to come back out of their zone anyway so there's no point in really chasing it.
Go straight to the bench for a quick line change since the clear is long enough to get fresh legs out onto the ice safely.
Attack hard — a PK icing or clear can become a shorthanded chance if you pursue it aggressively. Speed wins that race.
Wait for your penalty kill teammate to come up the ice with you so you can pressure the puck together as a tandem up there.
U9 / Novice.why: A PK clear that gets into the other team's zone is a chance for a shorthanded goal if you pursue aggressively. The other team is coming back for it — getting there first wins the race.
U9 / Novice.tip: PK clear going deep? Attack it. Speed wins the race and shorthanded goals are game-changers.

U9 / Novice.sit: Defending. Their forward has the puck in the corner. Another forward is parked in front of your net. Who do you cover?
opts: Both at the same time
The forward in front of your net — keep them off the goalie
The goalie
The forward in the corner — go fight for the puck
U9 / Novice.why: When the puck is in the corner, somebody else attacks it. Your job is the player in front of YOUR net — they are the dangerous one if a pass comes out.
U9 / Novice.tip: Cover the slot.

U9 / Novice.sit: A shooter comes down the wing. The goalie is hiding deep in the net. Where should the goalie be?
opts: Off to the side of the net
Way back in the net, near the goal line
Out a little, lined up with the puck
Skating up to challenge at the blue line
U9 / Novice.why: A goalie deep in the net leaves a huge open area on either side. Coming out a few feet — staying square to the puck — covers more of the net from the shooter's view.
U9 / Novice.tip: Square up.

U9 / Novice.sit: You fall down chasing a loose puck in the corner. Their forward picks it up. What do you do?
opts: Skate to the bench for a line change
Yell at your teammates to get it
Stay on the ice and rest a second
Get up as fast as you can and battle to get the puck back
U9 / Novice.why: Falling happens. What separates good players: how fast they get up. Pop up and battle — your team needs you, not your excuses.
U9 / Novice.tip: Get up, go.

U9 / Novice.sit: You are the first forward into the other team's zone. Their defenseman has the puck behind their net. What is your job?
opts: Skate hard at the puck-carrier and force them to make a quick decision
Stand at the blue line and wait
Chase their other defenseman
Skate to the front of their net
U9 / Novice.why: F1 (the first forward in) hunts the puck-carrier. Skate at them with speed and angle them so they have less time and space to make a clean play.
U9 / Novice.tip: Hunt the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're carrying the puck on a 2-on-1 rush. The defender is between you and your teammate. What's your best play?
opts: Shoot the puck at the goalie
Skate around the defender
Pass to your teammate on the right
Stop and wait for help
U11 / Atom.why: Look at the defender's stick — it's reaching toward your side, taking away your shot. The lane to your teammate on the right is wide open. When the defender plays the puck, the pass is the right play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Look at the defender's stick — it's reaching toward your side, taking away your shot.

U11 / Atom.tip: You're not picking the option you want — you're picking the option the defender is giving you.

False

False

False

False

opts: False
True
U11 / Atom.why: When the defender steps up, the gap is closing fast and you have very little time. Slowing down means he hits you. The right move is to get the puck off your stick fast — pass or chip.
U11 / Atom.tip: When the defender steps up, the gap is closing fast and you have very little time.

opts: Aggressive defenders are easy to skate around
Aggressive defenders are always good
Aggressive defenders create space behind them by stepping up
Aggressive defenders never give up space
U11 / Atom.why: Aggressive defenders create space behind them. The same speed that lets them attack you also means they can't stop and recover. Use their momentum against them — chip past, or pass to a teammate who's now wide open.
U11 / Atom.tip: Aggressive defenders create space behind them.

opts: True
False

U11 / Atom.tip: True.

Get a shot off before they catch you
Slow down to wait for support
Dump it in the corner
U11 / Atom.why: Time-pressure = release. A missed shot is way better than a stick lift that takes you out of the play entirely.
U11 / Atom.tip: Time-pressure = release.

Curl back behind the net
Skate up the middle through traffic
Look up the boards for your near-side winger
U11 / Atom.tip: Wall pass to your near-side winger is the shortest, safest first option..

Skate it through center yourself
Pass it to the goalie and quit on the play
Throw a blind pass up the middle

Shoot from the high blue line
Dump it in deep and forecheck

opts: Skate behind the net to slow the play down

Pass to the wide winger to pull a defender
Try to beat both defenders 1-on-2 down the middle
U11 / Atom.tip: When a defender hasn't committed, move the puck to the open side and make them..
U11 / Atom.why: When a defender hasn't committed, move the puck to the open side and make them.

U11 / Atom.sit: Of these, which is the best defensive habit?
opts: Stick blade vertical pointed at the carrier
Stick flat on the ice in the lane, ready to deflect a pass
Stick on your hip while you watch the puck
Stick raised in the air, ready to swing
U11 / Atom.why: A flat blade on the ice covers the lane all by itself. Raised, hipped, or vertical sticks give the carrier a free pass through space.
U11 / Atom.tip: A flat blade on the ice covers the lane all by itself.

media.alt: Stick blade is flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck carrier and the open teammate — taking away the pass without leaving the feet.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're down and out of position. The puck is sliding toward an open corner of the net and you can't get up in time. What do you try?
opts: Call for a penalty on the shooter — wave your glove at the ref because you were knocked down and ask for a delayed call on the play.
Stay still and trust the post — the puck usually hits the pipe from that angle, so freezing in place is the best chance to get lucky.
Give up the goal and reset — once you're down you can't recover, so accept it and get back in position for the next shift after the puck drop.
Throw your stick or extend whatever body part you can reach into the path of the puck — any contact can change its direction.
U11 / Atom.why: Out-of-position desperation is a real skill. Throwing your blocker hand, extending your toe, or reaching with your stick can deflect a puck that would have been a sure goal. Never give up on a play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Out of position? Reach with anything. A blocker or a toe can make a save.

U11 / Atom.sit: The point player is winding up for a shot and there's a screener standing right in your sightline. You can't see the puck. What do you do?
opts: Give up on the shot — you can't see it — if there's no way to track the puck just cover the low corners and hope it hits you for a lucky save.
Move laterally to find a gap around the screener, call to your D to move them, and be ready for a tip.
Drop into butterfly early to cover the bottom half — most screened shots go low because the shooter can't see either, so seal the ice completely.
Stay completely still and trust positioning — any movement takes you out of the angle, so hold your spot and let the puck hit you on the save.
U11 / Atom.why: Screeners require active problem-solving. Moving to find the puck, calling for your D to move the screener, and being ready for a tip is the full response. Passive goalies get scored on by screens.
U11 / Atom.tip: Screened shot: find the puck, call for help, expect a tip. Don't be passive.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team rushes 3-on-2. The two defenders take your two wingers. You're the trailing center. Where do you position?
opts: Stay high and be ready for a drop pass or rebound shot.
Skate to the corner to cover the weak-side winger.
Stop at the red line to backcheck early.
Race hard to the net for a tip-in chance.
U11 / Atom.why: The trailer on a 3-on-2 stays high — ready for a drop pass if the play breaks down or to get a rebound shot from the top.
U11 / Atom.tip: Trailer on a rush: stay high. You're the safety and the extra option.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team rushes 3-on-2. Both defenders take your two wingers. The middle lane is completely open. You're the center. What do you do?
opts: Pass across to the left winger for a clean shot at the net.
Pull up at the blue line and circle back for a full regroup.
Drive the middle lane and shoot — the defenders opened it for you.
Pass across to the right winger for a quick one-timer chance.
U11 / Atom.why: When both defenders go to the wings, they've given you the middle. Driving the lane and shooting catches the goalie off-guard.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both D go wide on a 3-on-2? Drive the middle. Shoot.

U11 / Atom.sit: The other team has the puck in your defensive zone. What's the most important thing every player on your team should be doing?
opts: Waiting for a turnover.
Watching the puck.
Skating to the blue.
Finding a player to cover.
U11 / Atom.why: In your defensive zone, every player needs to find a check. Watching the puck without picking up a check is how goals happen.
U11 / Atom.tip: Own zone: find your check first. Then watch the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck along the right boards. You're the defenseman. You want to angle them into the corner. Which direction should you push them?
opts: Toward the middle of the ice.
Into the corner toward.
It doesn't matter immediately.
Straight backward toward your net.
U11 / Atom.why: Angling means steering the puck carrier away from the dangerous middle and into the corner.
U11 / Atom.tip: Angling: push them to the boards, away from the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: You receive the puck at the half-wall. The goalie is slightly out of position and you have a shooting lane. What do you do?
opts: Shoot at the exposed net immediately.
Pass back to the defense at the point.
Hold the puck and look for an option.
Pass to the winger breaking to the net.
U11 / Atom.why: An out-of-position goalie with a shooting lane is an opportunity. Shoot before they recover.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie out of position with a lane? Shoot. Right now.

U11 / Atom.sit: Three attackers enter your zone. They overload the left side — two attackers on the left and one on the right. Both your D are back. How should they adjust?
opts: Both D go to the left to cover the two attackers — when the other team overloads one side, your defensemen should both shift there to match numbers.
One D goes behind the net to cover — pre-rotate to cover a pass behind the goal line to take away wraparound plays before they develop at all.
One D takes the two on the left, the other holds the middle and takes the weak-side attacker — both D communicate.
Both D shift to the right weak side — defend the back door because that's where the pass is coming to create a tap-in goal for the third attacker.
U11 / Atom.why: An overload means the D have to communicate and adjust. One D takes the strong side with two attackers, while the partner takes the weak-side player and the slot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Overload coverage: communicate. One takes the strong side, the other holds the middle and weak side.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck in the corner. Two defenders collapse. Your only open teammate is at the blue line, but the long pass is blocked. What do you do?
opts: Attempt the long stretch pass to the blue line and hope it sneaks through the two defenders.
Try to spin through both defenders with quick footwork and create your own lane out.
Shoot the puck hard around the boards and trust a teammate is there on the other side.
Protect the puck tight to the boards with your body, wait for the angle to open, then make a short chip pass.
U11 / Atom.why: Under dual pressure with a long pass blocked, protecting tight and waiting for an opening is the disciplined play. Two defenders can't both have perfect angles — one will leave a gap.
U11 / Atom.tip: Two defenders collapsing? Protect tight and wait. The gap will open.

U11 / Atom.sit: You enter the offensive zone planning to shoot right. But the goalie covers that side and a teammate breaks to the net on the left. What do you do?
opts: Dump it into the corner and send your linemates in to regroup and start the cycle.
Read and react — pass to your breaking teammate. The plan changes when the situation changes.
Slow down, circle back to the blue line, and think about what the best option might be.
Shoot to the right side as planned — the coach drew it up this way so stick to the plan.
U11 / Atom.why: Plans are starting points, not locks. If the situation changes, read it and react.
U11 / Atom.tip: Read what's actually there. Your plan is a starting point, not a commitment.

U11 / Atom.sit: Two forecheckers are deep in your zone. Your winger and center are both covered. Your D has the puck behind the net. What's the best option?
opts: Wheel and carry it out — both forecheckers are committed to your forwards. The D carrying it out is the best option.
Shoot the puck around the boards — rim it hard along the wall past the forecheckers and let your winger chase it up to the neutral zone.
Wait behind the net with the puck — hold it until the forecheckers commit to one side and then make your decision based on what they do.
Force a pass to a covered teammate — a quick hard pass can break through the coverage if you put it on the tape before the forechecker reacts.
U11 / Atom.why: When both forecheckers have committed to your forwards and left the D unguarded, the D carrying it out is the right play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both forecheckers on your forwards? D carry it out. The lane is open.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the power play. The penalty kill gets the puck and two players immediately break the other way 2-on-2. What should your team do?
opts: All five players retreat to the front of your own net.
Ignore the rush and stay in the offensive zone ready for the next chance.
One forward pressures the puck, the others recover their positions — don't panic.
Chase both attackers hard and try to attack and score on the rush the other way.
U11 / Atom.why: A power play counter-attack is dangerous but not a guaranteed goal. One player pressures the puck, everyone else recovers position. Panicking creates a turnover.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK breaks out on a counter? One pressures, others recover. Don't panic.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the D at the point. The opposing center is breaking toward your blue line for a counter. Your team still has the puck. What do you do?
opts: Ignore them completely and focus on keeping the puck in deep.
Hold at the blue line and let the formation develop deeper.
Call for the puck yourself so you can take the shot from the point.
Hold the blue line but cheat back slightly — be ready if the puck turns over.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading a player beginning to position for a counter-attack lets you cheat back slightly. You stay at the line but you're ready. If the puck turns, you're in position.
U11 / Atom.tip: Center positioning for counter? Cheat back slightly. Ready for the turnover.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your goalie stops a shot and has the puck. One winger is coming back and available. The forecheckers are still deep. What's the goalie's best move?
opts: Shoot it the length of the ice toward the far end.
Pass to the winger coming back — beat the forecheck.
Rim it hard around the boards past the forecheckers.
Freeze the puck and take the faceoff in your own zone.
U11 / Atom.why: A goalie handling the puck can start the breakout before the forecheck resets. A crisp pass to the returning winger beats the pressure.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie has it and a winger is coming back? Goalie starts the breakout. Beat the forecheck.

U11 / Atom.sit: Goalie makes a save and controls the puck. Three forecheckers are in your zone. Your wingers are pinned. What's the right breakout?
opts: Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player.
Goalie shoots the puck hard down the ice — a long clear past their blue line gets the puck out of danger and forces them to reset fully.
Goalie holds the puck and stops play for a faceoff — three forecheckers means breakout is too dangerous, so a faceoff is the safer play for your team.
Goalie rims the puck around the boards behind the net — the winger on the far side can pick it up as it comes around the far corner quick.
U11 / Atom.why: After a save under forecheck pressure, the goalie needs to read the breakout. Finding the center or an escaping winger breaks the forecheck immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie made the save under pressure? Find the breaking player. Read the breakout.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone for a long shift. The defenders are getting tired. What should your forwards be doing to take advantage?
opts: Shoot from every spot on the ice no matter the angle or lane.
Change lines right away before the tired defenders can recover their wind.
Keep cycling and creating traffic — tired defensemen make mistakes under sustained.
Slow the pace down so your own forwards can save their legs.
U11 / Atom.why: Sustained offensive zone pressure tires defensemen. Tired defenders make bad reads, miss sticks, and give up inside position.
U11 / Atom.tip: D getting tired in your zone? Keep the pressure going. Fatigue creates mistakes.

U11 / Atom.sit: You receive the puck in your own zone with a forechecker right on you. Your D partner is five feet away and completely open. What do you do?
opts: Wait for a better option to open.
Shoot the puck the length of the ice.
Try to skate out of the zone yourself.
Pass immediately to your open D partner.
U11 / Atom.why: The open D partner five feet away is the easy, correct play. Under pressure the simple play is almost always right.
U11 / Atom.tip: Open partner right beside you? Give it to them. Simple.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your power play just shot and the goalie covered. O-zone faceoff and you win the draw. Two of their forwards are already breaking out. What's the play?
opts: Hold the puck along the boards and regroup — let your power play catch up and set up the attack after you've re-entered the offensive zone clean.
Get the puck to a breaking teammate immediately — the 2-on-2 counter is better than resetting the PP.
Dump the puck in deep and chase — get it behind the defense and forecheck hard since you've got fresh legs on your power play unit already.
Set up the power play again and let your teammates handle everything — your PP is already working so you reset in the offensive zone and run it again.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading a counter-opportunity after a dead puck is advanced hockey sense. If two forwards are breaking and you win the draw, the counter is a better chance than resetting the power play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Win the draw and teammates are breaking? Go. Counter-attack beats a reset every time.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team forechecks 2-1-2. Both D and one forward are below the dots. You're the second high forward. The D rims the puck to the far winger. Who gets it?
opts: The near winger goes — they're in better position. You stay high and be the next pass option.
The D jumps up from below the dots to retrieve the rimmed puck.
Both forwards chase it hard together so one of you wins the race to the boards.
You go — you're the highest forward so the rimmed puck is your responsibility.
U11 / Atom.why: The near winger retrieves the rim their assignment. You stay high as the next option. If both forwards go deep, you lose your high forward safety.
U11 / Atom.tip: Rim going to the far winger? Let them get it. You stay high. Don't both go deep.

U11 / Atom.sit: You enter the offensive zone with speed. A defender is square in front of you, but your linemate is wide open on the left wing. What do you do?
opts: Try to deke around the defender one-on-one.
Slow down and wait for more teammate help.
Dump the puck in deep and chase after it.
Pass to your open linemate immediately.
U11 / Atom.why: The defender is committed to you — which means your teammate is open. Defender takes you = give it up.
U11 / Atom.tip: Defender takes you + teammate is open = pass. Almost always.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck in your own zone. A forechecker is right on you. Your D partner is five feet away and completely open. What do you do?
opts: Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
Pass immediately to your open D partner.
Try to skate out yourself past the forechecker.
Shoot the puck all the way down the ice.
U11 / Atom.why: The open D partner five feet away is the easy, correct play. Under pressure the simple play is almost always right.
U11 / Atom.tip: Open partner right beside you? Give it to them. Simple.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is attacking 3-on-2. Where should the three forwards position themselves?
opts: Two forwards go left wing and the third goes right wing.
One attacker goes in alone while the other two hang back at center ice.
Spread out across the ice so the two defenders can't cover everyone.
All three forwards skate together in a tight group up the middle.
U11 / Atom.why: Spreading out on a rush forces the defenders to make an impossible choice. A tight group lets two players cover all three.
U11 / Atom.tip: Rush attack: spread out. Make the defenders choose.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your defenseman has the puck behind the net. Both wingers are covered. The other defenseman is open on the far side. What's the best option?
opts: Walk out from behind the net alone and skate.
Shoot the puck hard around the boards and change.
Force a pass to a covered winger anyway.
Pass across to the open defenseman to reset the breakout.
U11 / Atom.why: When your outlets are covered, the D-to-D pass behind the net resets the play. It switches the point of attack and gives the other side time to get open.
U11 / Atom.tip: Outlets covered? Go D-to-D. Reset and rebuild.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. The puck is in the left corner. You're the right winger. Where's your best spot?
opts: Go into the left corner to help your teammate win the puck.
Stay on the right side near the net — the weak side.
Skate back to center ice so you're ready for a fast break.
Skate back to the blue line and be ready for a point shot.
U11 / Atom.why: As the weak-side winger, your job is to stay available on the opposite side. Pucks go cross-ice constantly — you need to be ready.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck in the far corner? Stay on your side near the net. That's where the cross-ice pass goes.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're cycling below the dots, low in the corner with the puck. A defender is on you. Your winger is at the half-wall. What do you do?
opts: Try to hold the puck alone in the corner and wait for help.
Pass to the half-wall winger and let the cycle continue.
Pass all the way back to the point to reset the play again.
Shoot from the corner and hope for a rebound near the net.
U11 / Atom.why: When you're pressured low in the corner, passing to the half-wall winger moves the puck to a player with more options — they can shoot, pass to the slot, or continue the cycle.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pressured in the corner? Pass to the half-wall. They have more options than you do.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck along the right boards toward your net. You're the defender. What's the goal of your positioning?
opts: Angle your body to steer them along the boards and away from the middle of the ice.
Wait for them to shoot from a bad angle and then block the shot with your body fast.
Stop them cold by skating straight at them and poking the puck off their stick quickly.
Back up all the way to your crease and let the goalie handle it.
U11 / Atom.why: Angling means steering the puck carrier where you want them — along the boards and away from the dangerous middle of the ice.
U11 / Atom.tip: Angling: push them to the boards. Keep them out of the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate is about to take a shot from the point. Nobody is near the net. What should you do right now?
opts: Back up to the blue line and be ready to defend a fast break.
Call for a pass instead of letting them take a long point shot.
Skate hard to the front of the net and get ready for a rebound.
Stay right where you are in case the shot misses wide of the net.
U11 / Atom.why: Getting to the net before the shot arrives means you're in position for a rebound before the goalie can cover it. Net-front presence wins games.
U11 / Atom.tip: Teammate about to shoot? Crash the net. Get there before the puck does.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck in your zone, pressured. Your teammate is breaking into the neutral zone — 40 feet away but wide open. What's the read?
opts: Dump the puck out along the boards and chase it yourself.
Skate it out yourself through the pressure.
Hold the puck behind the net and wait for a better option.
Attempt the stretch pass to the breaking teammate.
U11 / Atom.why: A stretch pass to a breaking forward turns a defensive zone puck into an immediate odd-man rush. The risk is the pass — but if the lane is open, it's the highest-reward play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Breaking teammate wide open? Thread the stretch pass. Big reward if you make it.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your D picks up the puck. Both wingers are tied up by forecheckers. But your center is open in the middle of the ice. What's the breakout call?
opts: Pass to the open center in the middle — they're clean.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far corner.
Force the pass to one of the covered wingers along the wall.
Carry the puck out yourself through the pressure.
U11 / Atom.why: The center in the middle is often the hidden option on a breakout. If both wingers are covered, the center up the middle is the smart outlet.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both wingers covered? The center in the middle might be the cleanest option.

U11 / Atom.sit: You don't have the puck in the offensive zone and your teammate is being pressured. The defender covering you is watching the puck. What do you do?
opts: Wait right where you are until the pressure clears so you don't crowd your teammate out.
Skate over toward your covered teammate to give them a close support pass option quickly.
Stand still in your spot and call loudly for the puck so your teammate knows you are open.
Skate to a new open spot — cut to the net or find a seam. Your defender will have to follow.
U11 / Atom.why: Moving without the puck forces your defender to follow you — and every step they take to follow you is a step away from the puck carrier. Create problems by moving.
U11 / Atom.tip: No puck and defender watching the play? Move. Make them follow you. Create space.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your D picks up the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are coming but they haven't arrived yet. You have 2 seconds. What's the key thing your D should do?
opts: Look up immediately and read which outlet is open before the pressure arrives — decide early.
Rim the puck around the boards no matter what the forecheckers are doing on that play.
Wait until both forecheckers have arrived before deciding which outlet pass to try to make.
Skate out of the zone with the puck no matter what pressure is coming from the forecheckers.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading the breakout before the pressure arrives — deciding early — is the difference between a clean exit and getting trapped. The D needs to see the ice before the forechecker takes the options away.
U11 / Atom.tip: D with the puck and pressure coming? Look up and decide early. Don't wait.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with two minutes left. You win a faceoff in the offensive zone. What's most important?
opts: Control the puck, cycle below the dots, use every second — only shoot on a high-quality chance.
Dump the puck deep into the far corner and chase it hard with all your forwards.
Take a big slap shot immediately on the faceoff draw to try to extend your lead early.
Pass the puck all the way back to your own zone to be safe and kill more clock.
U11 / Atom.why: Late-game puck management is a skill. Cycling below the dots forces the other team to chase, burns clock, and keeps the puck away from their skilled players.
U11 / Atom.tip: Leading late: the clock is your ally. Protect the puck. Make them chase.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're defending in your zone. An attacker cuts left-to-right across the net. Your partner had them, but now they're on your side. What do you do?
opts: Call for the referee to whistle a penalty on the crossing play.
Ignore the attacker completely and trust that your partner will just follow them over.
Back up all the way to the crease and let the attacker set up wherever they want to go.
Call switch and take the attacker coming to your side so your partner can recover.
U11 / Atom.why: When an attacker cuts across in front of the net, the defending pair needs to communicate a switch. You take the player coming to your side — your partner recovers.
U11 / Atom.tip: Attacker cuts to your side? Call switch and take them. Let your partner recover.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your defenseman picks up the puck in your own corner. As a winger, where should you be going?
opts: Stand in the corner with the defenseman and help.
Get up the wall to give the D an outlet option.
Skate straight to center ice and call for a pass.
Stay close to the net in case of a loose rebound.
U11 / Atom.why: Breakout positioning means getting to spots that give your defenseman real options. As a winger, that's typically up the wall.
U11 / Atom.tip: D has the puck in the corner? Get up the wall. Give them an out.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate is about to shoot from the half-wall. The goalie is set. You're the weak-side winger. What should you be doing?
opts: Call loudly for a pass instead of the shot so you can get a better chance from your side.
Back up all the way to your own blue line to protect against a breakaway the other way.
Stay right where you are in case the shot misses wide and comes out to your side cleanly.
Crashing the net hard on the weak side — be there for the rebound before the goalie can cover.
U11 / Atom.why: The weak-side winger crashing the net on a shot is one of the highest-value plays without the puck. Rebounds bounce weak side — being there before the shot lands is how goals are scored.
U11 / Atom.tip: Teammate shooting? Crash the net. Weak-side rebound is your job.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your PP is entering the o-zone. Two penalty killers are set at their blue line. How should your team attack?
opts: Skate back into your own zone and regroup over and over until the PK gets tired and breaks.
Dump the puck deep into the corner and send all five skaters in hard to chase and recover it.
Carry the puck straight up the middle every time since you have the extra skater advantage anyway.
Use the extra skater advantage: hold the puck until one PK player commits, then move it to the open player.
U11 / Atom.why: The extra skater on the power play means someone is always open. Don't just carry into a set defense — hold until a PK player commits, then move the puck to who they left open.
U11 / Atom.tip: PP zone entry vs. set PK: hold until they commit. Then use the extra skater.

U11 / Atom.sit: You just won a neutral-zone faceoff. The puck goes back to your D. You're the center. What now?
opts: Skate straight back to the bench for a quick line change because the puck is already safe with your defenseman and your shift is basically over, so the next line can handle the rush.
Transition immediately to your assignment — if you won the draw, get to your position in the rush development. The faceoff result gives your team the advantage if everyone executes.
Stand completely still at the faceoff dot and watch what your defenseman decides to do with the puck before you even consider making any kind of next move up the ice at all.
Celebrate winning the draw with your linemates and tap sticks, because winning a neutral-zone faceoff is a big deal and your whole team should enjoy the moment before the next play.
U11 / Atom.why: Winning a faceoff is just the start — executing the assignment immediately after is what turns a faceoff win into a scoring chance. The center transitions to their rush role.
U11 / Atom.tip: Win the faceoff? Execute your next assignment immediately. Don't watch — go.

U11 / Atom.q: Which position is the highlighted green-team player?
U11 / Atom.tip: Center plays the middle — wings on the boards, D behind, G in net.

U11 / Atom.q: Which position is the highlighted green-team player?
U11 / Atom.tip: Wings cover the boards on their side. Facing your goalie, LW is on your left.

U11 / Atom.q: Which position is the highlighted green-team player?
U11 / Atom.tip: Wings cover the boards on their side. Facing your goalie, RW is on your right.

U11 / Atom.q: Which position is the highlighted green-team player?
U11 / Atom.tip: Defense play behind the forwards. Facing your goalie, LD is on your left.

U11 / Atom.q: Which position is the highlighted green-team player?
U11 / Atom.tip: Defense play behind the forwards. Facing your goalie, RD is on your right.

U11 / Atom.q: Which position is the highlighted green-team player?
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalies stay in or near the crease — only player allowed there.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You enter the offensive zone on a 2-on-1. The defender has committed his stick and body weight to the puck side. What's the highest-percentage play?
opts: Drive wide and protect the puck
Chip and chase past the defender
Shoot — the goalie is square and might be beatable
Pass cross-ice — your teammate has an open lane
U13 / Peewee.why: Defender stick is on the puck side — he's taking away your shot. Your teammate has a clean cross-ice lane to a goalie who has to push across the crease. Pass creates a 50%+ scoring chance vs. ~10% for a shot against a square goalie. Textbook 2-on-1 read: read what he's taking away, do the opposite.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender stick is on the puck side — he's taking away your shot.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U13 / Peewee.sit: True or False: A shot that goes in the net was the right play, even if a pass would have created a better chance.
opts: True
False
U13 / Peewee.why: Outcomes lie. A bad decision can produce a good result, and a great decision can produce a bad result. In hockey, the read is judged by what was available, not what happened. A goal off a bad shot is still a bad shot. A missed pass that should have been a goal is still the right read.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Outcomes lie.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U13 / Peewee.sit: True or False: The right play here is to shoot.
