U13 / Peewee.why: On a PK defensive zone faceoff, winning it forward — toward the boards or corner — takes away the PP's plan. Winning backward gives the PP exactly what they want.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK faceoff in your own zone: win it forward. Take away the point shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Three attackers are rushing at your two defensemen. All three are at top speed. What's the biggest mistake a defenseman can make here?
opts: Hold the puck and then get back into your defensive position at that point.
Hold the puck since the other team has committed to the other side instead.
Backing up at the right controlled pace, not overcommitting.
Collapse toward the puck immediately without maintaining your gap.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pinching up against a fast rush hands the attacking team a massive speed advantage. Backing up at the right controlled pace is the correct response.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rush coming with speed? Back up controlled. Never pinch up against a fast rush.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 90 seconds left. You win a puck battle in the neutral zone. What's the right thing to do with the puck?
opts: Set up behind the net and wait and then get back into your defensive position while staying in position.
Cut to the middle of the ice because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice instead.
Shoot from wherever you are now because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Control the puck along the boards, protect it, and force the other team to come get it — use every second.
U13 / Peewee.why: Game management late means using the clock. Protecting the puck in the neutral zone makes the other team come to you and burns precious seconds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Leading late and won the puck? Protect it. Use the clock. Make them come to you.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is forechecking and the other team wins a battle in the corner. They're about to break out with speed. You're the high forward. What's the most important thing you do right now?
opts: Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
Hold your position and intercept the first outlet pass — you're the plug.
Skate to the corner and dig it out before they react before they score.
Skate to the corner and dig it out from the point and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the puck changes in the corner, the high forward's job is to be the first interceptor — not to chase. Holding position and reading the breakout gives you a chance to pick off the first pass.
U13 / Peewee.tip: They won the corner battle? High forward reads the breakout. Don't chase — intercept.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your PP is in a 1-3-1. The half-wall player has the puck. The near PK forward is cheating toward the half-wall. The seam pass to the middle is open. What's the play?
opts: Thread the seam pass to the middle player — the PK forward cheating toward you opened that lane.
Pull up at the hash marks because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice first.
Change lines because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
Back up and defend because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the PK forward cheats toward you, they've vacated the seam. The middle player is open. The seam pass is the highest-danger play.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK forward cheated toward you? The seam is open. Thread it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team just changed lines mid-play in the neutral zone. Their new players are still getting up to speed. You have the puck. What does this create?
opts: Force a play through traffic rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
A transition window — attack immediately while their fresh players aren't at full speed or in position.
Force a play through traffic because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: A line change mid-play creates a brief window where the fresh players aren't at full speed or position. Attacking immediately exploits that window before it closes.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Other team just changed mid-play? Attack now. Their fresh players aren't set yet.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're skating at full speed toward the net. A defender is chasing from behind. Should you slow down to take a more accurate shot?
opts: Yes — stop completely and shoot from a still position.
Yes — slow down and take a more accurate wrist shot.
Yes — slow down, cut across the crease, then shoot.
No — shoot while moving at full speed, making it harder for the goalie.
U13 / Peewee.why: Shooting while moving at full speed is harder for a goalie to track. Slowing down also lets the defender catch up. Shoot while moving.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Full speed toward the net with a defender behind you? Shoot moving. Don't slow down.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in the o-zone with a decent but not great shot angle. Your teammate in the slot is slightly covered but has a much better shot. What do you do?
opts: Force a play and risk losing the puck to maintain possession.
Pass to the slot player — the higher danger location is worth the slight coverage risk.
Get your stick in the lane since forcing it would be risky here and look for support.
Get your stick in the lane because your teammates need time to set up from this spot.
U13 / Peewee.why: A slightly covered player in the slot still represents a higher-danger chance than a clean shot from a poor angle. The math of chance quality favors the slot, even with some risk.
U13 / Peewee.tip: OK angle vs. slightly covered slot? The slot wins. Higher danger is worth the risk.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You catch a pass in the slot and see an opening top-corner. You hesitate two seconds repositioning the puck. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
opts: Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it.
Look around and wait for more options rather than shooting immediately.
Pass back because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice early.
Skate hard rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Hesitating in the slot gives the goalie time to recover. If there's an opening when you catch the puck, release immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Opening in the slot? Release immediately. The best shot is the first one.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You receive a pass at the blue line on the power play. A penalty killer is closing fast. The slot is open. You have one second. What's right?
opts: Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
Pass the puck back to the other point.
One-time it or shoot immediately into the open slot.
Skate into the zone yourself with it.
U13 / Peewee.why: One second on the power play point with the slot open is a shooting opportunity — not a thinking opportunity. The PK player closing on you takes that lane away in two seconds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Point with an open shot? Release it fast. The lane closes quick.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. The power play passes to the point. You're the near penalty killer forward. What do you do?
opts: Back all the way up to your own crease to help the goalie see shots through heavy traffic.
Close on the point player to pressure the shot or take away the dangerous pass options.
Skate all the way into the corner to help your teammate battle for the loose puck down low.
Stay in the middle of the ice to block the cross-seam pass and protect the slot area well.
U13 / Peewee.why: Closing on the point player on the PK takes away a clean shooting lane or forces a rushed decision. You don't have to get the puck — just make them uncomfortable.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK: close on the point player. Make it hard for them to get a clean shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a power play. You're standing in front of the goalie. Your teammate at the point winds up for a shot. What's your job?
opts: Skate out of the way so the goalie can see the puck clearly and make an easy save there.
Skate to the corner to get position early for the rebound off the boards.
Stay planted and block the goalie's sightline — screen them, and be ready to tip the shot.
Call loudly for a pass instead of the shot so you can score from a better angle.
U13 / Peewee.why: A screener in front of the net on the power play is one of the highest-value positions. You block the goalie's view and tip shots combination creates goals.
U13 / Peewee.tip: In front of the net on the PP? Screen the goalie and tip the shot. Stay planted.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the defenseman and the opposing team's winger wins a puck battle in the corner in your defensive zone. They're about to pass to someone in the slot. What do you do?
opts: Skate down into the corner and try to re-win the puck battle from the opposing winger.
Watch the puck carrier closely and only react after the pass has already been made.
Skate to your blue line and hope your teammate wins the battle in the corner.
Seal off the passing lane to the slot — get between the puck carrier and the dangerous area.
U13 / Peewee.why: Once the puck battle is lost, the priority shifts to cutting off the next play — which is almost always a slot pass. Get between the puck carrier and the slot immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Lost the puck battle? Don't chase the puck. Seal the passing lane to the slot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is sitting back in a 1-2-2 neutral zone setup. Every time your team tries to enter, they get stopped at the red line. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Dump the puck deep every single rush and grind them down with a heavy forecheck eventually.
Pull the puck back to your defensemen, use a D-to-D pass to overload one side, or try a stretch pass to a breaking forward.
Skate around the outside of the trap along the boards and curl back into the middle with speed.
Keep carrying the puck in at full speed until the trap finally breaks down and lets you by.
U13 / Peewee.why: A 1-2-2 trap is designed to stop straight-line carries. The answer is patience and lateral movement.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Against a trap: go backward to go forward. Change the angle. Be patient.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is taking a defensive zone faceoff. Before the puck drops, what should every player already know?
opts: Their assignment — who they're covering and where they're going based on whether you win or lose the draw.
To skate as fast as they possibly can toward the puck the moment it hits the ice surface.
Nothing at all — players should just react to where the puck goes after the official drops it.
Where the nearest door to the bench is in case their shift ends right after the puck drops.
U13 / Peewee.why: A faceoff is a set play. Every player should know their assignment before the puck drops — what to do if you win, what to do if you lose. The setup before the drop is as important as the drop itself.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Before the faceoff drops: know your assignment. Win or lose — be ready to execute.

U13 / Peewee.sit: An opposing winger broke out and is coming at you with speed. You're at the red line. They're fast and have a step on you. What's your priority?
opts: Charge right at them and try to take the puck off their stick before they can get across the red line and into your zone.
Stand completely still at the red line and wait for them to come to you before you commit to any kind of defensive move.
Back up at a pace that doesn't let them get speed on you, angle them toward the boards, and wait.
Turn around and race them straight back to your net to try to beat them to the front before they can fire off a shot.
U13 / Peewee.why: An attacker with speed is trying to blow past you. Backing up at the right pace while angling keeps you ahead of them and denies the clean entry.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Fast attacker with a step on you? Back up fast and angle. Don't let them get speed going.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the last defender on a 2-on-1. Goalie is set. One attacker has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
opts: Chase the cutter all the way to the right post to make sure they can't tap in any pass that comes across to the back door.
Back up all the way to your own crease so you're right next to the goalie and can cover any rebound that comes out on a shot.
Commit hard to the puck carrier on the left and try to take the puck off their stick before they can make any kind of play.
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Last defender back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass, force the shot, trust your goalie. Don't commit to either attacker until they force your hand.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Last defender back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass. Let the goalie have the shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1 and has been set up for 30 seconds without generating a shot. Two PK forwards are pressuring the top. What should the top player do?
opts: Take a slap shot from distance through all the traffic and hope for a rebound chance.
Move the puck quickly across to the half-wall before both pressure players arrive.
Pass the puck all the way back to your own zone and start the whole power play over.
Try to hold the puck and fight through both PK forwards to get a shot from the top.
U13 / Peewee.why: Two PK forwards pressuring one point means the rest of the formation has a gap — the half-wall is almost always where that gap is. Move the puck before both arrive.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two PKers coming? Move the puck before they get there. They can't cover two spots at once.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A skilled forward is coming at you 1-on-1 at full speed. They're a good stickhandler and have already made two players fall down tonight. What do you avoid doing?
opts: Backing up and staying patient until they make their move.
Waiting for their first move before reacting.
Poking check from maximum distance or charging them.
Angling them toward the boards and closing your gap.
U13 / Peewee.why: A skilled stickhandler wants you to commit early — a premature poke check gives them exactly the step they need to blow past you. Stay patient and contained.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Skilled forward 1-on-1? Don't commit early. Stay patient. Make them decide first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're at center with the puck on a 3-on-2. Wingers wide left and right, a third forward trailing. Both defenders are inside. What's the read?
opts: Look at both defenders first — pass to whichever winger the defenders are leaving more open, or drive the middle if it's open.
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone and chase it down with all three forwards to start a forecheck right now.
Shoot the puck from center ice right at the goalie to try to surprise them and maybe sneak one in through traffic.
Pass immediately to the left winger because that's the first open option you see and quick passes are always the right play.
U13 / Peewee.why: On a 3-on-2, reading both defenders first tells you which option is actually open before you commit. The best puck carrier reads the whole ice, not just one option.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 3-on-2 developing? Read both defenders before picking your option. Don't just default to one side.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck. The forechecker is coming from the left. Your left winger is covered. Your right winger is also covered. Your center is late getting back. What's the option?
opts: Force a hard pass to one of the covered wingers and hope they can win the battle down the wall.
Shoot the puck down the ice and take the icing whistle to get a fresh faceoff in your own zone.
Reverse behind the net — the forechecker on the left has to follow, which buys time for the center to get back.
Carry the puck straight out yourself and try to skate through the forechecker coming from the left side.
U13 / Peewee.why: When all outlets are covered and a forechecker is coming from one side, reversing behind the net forces the forechecker to follow — buying a few seconds for outlets to get open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: All outlets covered and a forechecker coming? Reverse behind the net. Buy time.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You win an o-zone faceoff. The other center had to tie the draw. The puck goes back to your D at the point. Before their center recovers, what do you do?
opts: Skate all the way over to the corner and see what kind of play develops down low before you commit to any real position.
Drive hard to the net immediately — the opposing center just spent all their energy on the draw and the lane to the net may be briefly open.
Wait patiently for the play to develop normally before you decide what to do with your positioning in the offensive zone right now.
Call for a pass from the defenseman at the point so you can get the puck back and try to set up a play in the high slot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Winning a faceoff in the offensive zone creates a split-second window where the opposing center is off-balance. Driving hard to the net immediately exploits that window before they recover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Win the OZ faceoff? Drive the net immediately. The center just fought the draw — they're recovering.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck. Your D has the puck and is looking to pass. You're a winger and you're covered. When should you break to the wall?
opts: Skate to the wall hard, using your speed to lose the forechecker for a split second — make the D's decision easy.
Don't break to the wall until the forechecker has already left your area and given up the chase.
Stand completely still in your spot and call loudly for the puck to get your D's attention.
Stay right where you are and trust that your defenseman will figure out the play on their own.
U13 / Peewee.why: Creating separation from your forechecker — even for a split second — is how you get open. Skate hard to the wall to create the separation that makes the pass possible.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Covered on the breakout? Skate hard to create separation. Make yourself open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: There's a loose puck in your defensive zone right in front of your net. You and an opposing player are racing to it at the same time. What's your priority?
opts: Get to the puck first and try to skate it all the way out of the zone yourself before the opponent can close on you.
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and get the puck while you block the opposing player from reaching the front of the net.
Get your body between the opponent and the puck — inside position wins this battle even if you get there second.
Poke at the puck with your stick from a distance and try to knock it loose without getting into the physical battle at all.
U13 / Peewee.why: Inside position on a loose puck in front of your own net wins the battle even if you get there a step late. Body between opponent and puck the priority.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Loose puck in your own zone? Get inside position. Body between opponent and puck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck carrier has the puck in the offensive zone but is being smothered by defenders. You don't have the puck and a defender is near you. What's the best thing you can do?
opts: Go help the puck carrier in the battle for the puck by skating right into the pile to try to get the puck loose for the team.
Skate into open space — cut to the net, move to a seam. Make yourself available AND drag your defender out of the puck carrier's area.
Skate out to the blue line right away so you're ready to make a breakout pass if the other team gets the puck back and clears.
Stand still and call for the puck loudly so the puck carrier knows exactly where you are and can make the pass to you.
U13 / Peewee.why: Off-puck movement does two things: it makes you available for a pass and it drags your defender away from the puck carrier — creating more space for them.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Puck carrier in trouble? Move without the puck. Create space for them by dragging your defender away.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your penalty kill clears the puck down the ice. Icing is called and the faceoff comes back to your defensive zone. Your two PK players are tired. What matters most before the next faceoff?
opts: Get your two best faceoff players and PK coverage players out — fresh legs and the right assignments matter more than speed.
Stay out on the ice because the familiar lineup is better than rolling new players out cold without any time to prepare or set.
Change lines immediately even if it causes confusion in the bench area because the tired players really do need a rest right away.
Send out the power play line instead because they have the best sticks on the team and can probably win the faceoff cleanly.
U13 / Peewee.why: A faceoff after an icing on the PK is a chance to reset with the right personnel. Getting the right PK players and faceoff specialist on the ice matters more than rushing.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK icing faceoff? Get the right players out. Fresh legs and right assignments.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the defenseman at the offensive blue line. Your winger just gained possession in the corner. Should you pinch to join the attack below the dots?
opts: Yes — always pinch hard and join every attack so you can keep the puck deep in the zone.
Yes — pinch every single time your winger touches the puck, no matter who is covering behind you.
Only if the other winger or center is positioned to cover the blue line behind you — otherwise hold the line.
No — never pinch from the blue line under any circumstances because it is always too risky.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pinching is right only when someone is positioned behind you to cover the blue line. Pinching without coverage gives up a breakout against you if the puck turns over.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Want to pinch? Make sure someone covers the line behind you first. Then go.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is entering 4-on-4. Your team is set up in the neutral zone — all four between the puck and your net. The puck carrier comes at you. What's the key?
opts: Everyone chase the puck carrier at the same time to surround them and force a quick turnover.
One player steps up to challenge while the other three retreat all the way back to the crease.
All four players skate backwards to your own blue line to protect the net area immediately.
Hold your positions and make the puck carrier make a decision — force them one way and take away the easiest options.
U13 / Peewee.why: A neutral zone defensive structure works when everyone holds their lane and forces the puck carrier into a decision. If everyone chases, the structure collapses.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Neutral zone defense: hold your lane. Force the puck carrier to decide. Don't all chase.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shot comes from the blue line. You're standing in front of the net. The puck is coming at the middle of the net right at the goalie. What's your job?
opts: Tip the puck — even a slight redirection completely changes the trajectory and defeats the goalie's.
Get out of the way fast so your goalie has a completely clear look at the incoming shot from the point.
Call loudly for the shot to go wide of the net so the puck rims around for a rebound chance.
Skate away from the blue paint quickly so you don't block your goalie's view of the incoming shot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Tipping a shot on net is one of the most effective plays in hockey. Even a small deflection changes the trajectory enough to beat a goalie who is tracking the original shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shot coming from the point right at the goalie? Tip it. Any deflection beats their tracking.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has been hemmed in your own zone for 45 seconds. Your D picks up the puck. Before they do anything else, what's the single most important question they should answer?
opts: What is the current score of the game and how much time is left on the clock right now?
Where is the nearest bench door so I can get a fresh change of players out on the ice now?
What did the coach say about the breakout during the pre-game meeting in the locker room?
Which outlet is actually open — not which one I want to use, but which one is actually free?
U13 / Peewee.why: Under pressure after a long zone time, the temptation is to default to a favorite outlet. But reading which option is actually open — not which one you want — is what leads to a clean exit.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Long zone time, D finally has the puck? Read what's actually open. Don't default.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're in your own end with a questionable pass available through the middle. Right call?
opts: Skate through two defenders.
Force the middle pass.
Glass-and-out — don't force it.
Reverse to your goalie.
U15 / Bantam.why: In your own end, the middle is sacred. If you aren't 100% sure, glass it out and regroup in the neutral zone.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Own end middle = only if certain.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Late in the game, up by one, you gain the offensive zone. A risky cross-ice pass is open. What's the smart play?
opts: Shoot from the blue line.
Force the cross-ice pass.
Skate into the defender.
Cycle the puck low and kill time.
U15 / Bantam.why: Up a goal late = protect possession. Cycle low, eat clock, force them to chase.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Up late? Cycle, don't gamble.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Shooters in your league have started targeting your glove side high consistently. You've given up three glove-side goals in the last two games. How do you adjust?
opts: Work on your glove positioning in practice — start with the glove slightly higher and more visible to shooters to take away that visual cue for targeting.
Tell your D to never let anyone shoot from your glove side since it's their job to take away that angle and any goal scored glove side high is a instead.
Switch to catching with your blocker hand since your glove side is clearly a weakness and changing hands will confuse the shooters who have been instead.
Don't change anything since three goals is a small sample size and adjusting your technique based on a few goals will create new problems in other areas.
U15 / Bantam.why: Adjusting glove positioning is the right response. Starting slightly higher takes away the visual cue shooters are using. Address the weakness in practice.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Getting beaten glove side? Adjust the starting position. Work it in practice.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck is dumped in behind your net. You leave to play it. A forechecker is closing. Your D is on the boards. What do you communicate?
opts: Stay silent and make the play yourself since talking behind the net can distract your defensemen.
Call the play — 'Leave it!' if you have time, or 'Rim it!' to your D if pressure is too close.
Don't communicate anything specific and let your D figure out on their own what the right call is.
Yell 'Help!' to get all your teammates to come back behind the net and assist with the puck.
U15 / Bantam.why: Goalie communication behind the net directs traffic. 'Leave it' tells the D you've got it. 'Rim' tells them you need them to take it. Clear calls prevent confusion.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Behind the net: direct traffic. 'Leave it' or 'Rim.' Be specific.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A point shot is coming and you see a forward in front with their stick on the ice, ready to tip. What do you prepare for?
opts: Drop into a full butterfly before the shot even arrives to cover the bottom of the net since deflections usually go low and you need to.
Ignore the forward and focus only on the original shot from the point since the deflection might not happen and you can't prepare.
Expect the deflection — stay low in your stance with your stick on the ice and track the puck through the screen and forward's deflection.
Stand up taller to see over the forward since a higher stance gives you a better view of the point shot as it comes through the traffic.
U15 / Bantam.why: A forward with their stick on the ice is a deflection setup. Stay low, track through, and expect the direction change. Deflections change angle and elevation.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Stick on the ice in front? Expect the tip. Stay low. Track through.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team just got a new coach mid-season who runs a completely different system than you're used to. You're struggling to execute it. What's the right approach?
opts: Keep playing the old system since it worked and the new coach should adapt to what the team already knows how to do instead of changing.
Refuse to buy into the new system and tell your teammates it won't work since the old coach's system was better and the new one doesn't fit.
Only run the new system when the coach is watching and go back to the old one during games since the old system is what the team is.
Commit fully to learning the new system even if it feels uncomfortable — watch video, ask questions, and practice the details every single rep.
U15 / Bantam.why: Buying into a new system is a character test. Even when it's uncomfortable, committing fully — video, questions, practice — is how good players adapt.
U15 / Bantam.tip: New system? Commit fully. Watch video. Ask questions. Discomfort is growth.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're F1 on the forecheck. The D picks up the puck and starts to skate behind their net. How do you approach?
opts: Angle your skating to cut off one side and force them toward your F2.
Slow down and wait to see what they do before committing to any.
Skate directly at them at full speed to try to get a big hit.
Peel off and go back to the neutral zone since they have possession.
U15 / Bantam.why: Angling as F1 takes away options. Cut off one side and force them into your support.
U15 / Bantam.tip: F1 forecheck: angle and cut off one side. Force them into support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D and an opposing forward is setting up in front of your net for a screen or deflection. What do you do?
opts: Ignore them and focus only on the puck carrier since that's.
Tie up their stick and box them out of the prime scoring area there.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
Push them hard from behind with both hands to get them away.
U15 / Bantam.why: Net-front defense is about stick work and body position. Tie up their stick and box them out.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Opponent at your net front? Tie up stick. Box out. Protect goalie's eyes.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your coach runs a 2-1-2 forecheck. You're one of the two forwards going in first. How does this differ from a 1-2-2?
opts: You go in alone first and your partner hangs back at the red line area.
You both stay at center ice and wait for them to bring the puck to you.
You and your partner pressure together aggressively to create turnovers.
You pinch from the blue line while your partner forechecks alone there.
U15 / Bantam.why: In a 2-1-2, two forwards pressure together. More aggressive than 1-2-2 with immediate support.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 2-1-2: two in, pressure together. More aggressive with immediate support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has the puck in the O-zone and it gets turned over. You're the nearest forward. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Skate straight to the bench for a change since your offensive shift.
Chase the puck carrier all the way back into your defensive zone alone.
Stand still and watch what the other team does before you react.
Apply immediate pressure on the new puck carrier to force a quick error.
U15 / Bantam.why: Immediate pressure on the turnover is the first line of defense. Force a quick decision before they break out.
U15 / Bantam.tip: OZ turnover? Pressure immediately. Force a bad decision.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're having a great shift creating chances. But you've been on the ice for over a minute. What do you do?
opts: Get off the ice for a change — tired players make costly mistakes.
Keep playing until you score since you are the best option out.
Don't say anything — let your play speak for itself.
Wait until the puck is in the O-zone and then stay on.
U15 / Bantam.why: Shift discipline matters more than individual performance. Tired players make turnovers that cost goals.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Great shift but tired? Get off. Discipline over heroics.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck carrier enters the neutral zone with speed. You're the D. They're fast. How does their speed change your gap?
opts: Same gap regardless of how fast the puck carrier is skating at.
Slightly bigger gap so they can't blow past you with their speed.
Tighter gap because fast players need to be challenged before.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a fast puck carrier, a slightly bigger gap prevents getting caught flat-footed.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Fast carrier? Slightly bigger gap. Don't get caught flat-footed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is killing a penalty. The opposing D has the puck in their zone setting up their PP breakout. What's your approach as the forechecker?
opts: Stand still at your blue line and let them walk the puck up unchecked.
Drop into a 1-3 NZ trap and dare them to dump it in.
Controlled pressure — take away the easy first pass without going deep.
Send both forwards hard on the forecheck to force a turnover in their zone.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK forechecking is controlled pressure. Take away the easy pass without overcommitting.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK forecheck: controlled pressure. Don't get caught deep.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is breaking out cleanly. You're a D and the forwards have speed through the neutral zone. There's a trailer lane open. Should you jump into the rush?
opts: No — both D should always stay back regardless of how clean the breakout looks.
Wait until the forwards dump the puck in and then join the forecheck as the fifth.
Only jump if the other team has already committed all their players back into their zone.
Yes — fill the trailer lane and give your team an extra option in the offensive zone attack.
U15 / Bantam.why: When the breakout is clean and there's a trailer lane, an activating D creates an overload in the offensive zone. The key is reading when it's safe.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Clean breakout with a trailer lane? Jump in. Extra option in the attack.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team's PP is set up in the zone. The PK is in a tight box. Every pass to the slot is getting intercepted. What do you do?
opts: Move the puck around the perimeter faster to force the box to shift and create the seam.
Have all five players crash the net at the same time to create chaos in front.
Pull the puck out of the zone and re-enter to reset since the formation isn't.
Keep forcing passes to the slot since eventually one will get through if you throw instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: A tight PK box is beaten by fast puck movement around the perimeter. Force them to shift — the seam opens when they can't rotate fast enough.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tight PK box? Move the puck faster around the perimeter. Force them to shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're one of two D on a 3-on-2 rush. The three attackers are spread across the ice. The puck is in the middle. What's your read?
opts: Both D back up to the goal line together and let the goalie handle everything.
You take the wide player on your side and trust your partner to take the other and play shot.
Both D collapse to the middle to take away the shot and leave the wide players for your.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: On a 3-on-2, each D takes a wide player. The middle player (shooter) is the goalie's responsibility. Collapsing both D to the middle leaves two wide options open.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 3-on-2: each D takes a wide player. Goalie plays the shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck through the neutral zone at you. You're the lone D back. Where should your gap be?
opts: At the far blue line so you can close the distance with a big.
Back up to your own goal line to give yourself maximum time to react.
One stick length at the top of the circles to force a quick decision.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: A tight gap at the top of the circles forces the attacker to make their move early. Too far back gives them time and space.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Gap control: top of the circles. Force their hand early.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D at the offensive blue line. The puck is dumped into the corner by your forward. The opposing D gets to it first. You see their winger starting to leak out. What do you do?
opts: Leave the blue line completely and go help your forward in the corner win the puck instead.
Skate backward to center ice to give yourself a head start in case the puck comes back out.
Pinch into the corner to try to force a turnover since your forward needs help winning it.
Hold the blue line and be ready to close on the winger if they try to exit on your side.
U15 / Bantam.why: With a winger leaking out, holding the line is critical. If you pinch and the puck gets to that winger, it's a breakaway.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Winger leaking out? Hold the line. Don't pinch into a breakaway against.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're on the PP half-wall. The puck is at the point. You see the PK is cheating toward the point shooter. The back-door player on the far side is open. What do you call for?
opts: The cross-ice pass from the point to the far side for the one-timer since the PK shifted away.
The puck back to the D in the corner to reset and try a new play since this look isn't.
A pass to you on the half-wall since you're in a better position to shoot from your.
A shot from the point through traffic since that's the highest percentage play from that instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: When the PK shifts toward the point, the far side opens up. A cross-ice pass for a one-timer exploits that shift perfectly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK shifting to the point? Cross-ice one-timer on the far side. Exploit the shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The opposing team is cycling in your zone. The puck carrier is below the goal line. You're the weak-side D. Where should your eyes be?
opts: Only on the puck carrier since you need to see what they do before you can react to.
On the weak-side player and the slot — the most dangerous pass is coming to your side next.
On your goalie to make sure they're set and tracking the puck through all the traffic.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: The weak-side D must have eyes on the weak-side player and the slot. The cross-ice pass or back-door play is the most dangerous option when the puck is below the goal line.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Weak-side D during a cycle: eyes on the weak-side player and slot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team's forecheck pressure has been working — you're getting turnovers. Entering the zone, a defender backs off and gives you space at the blue line. Do you carry or dump?
opts: Stop at the blue line and wait for all five players to get set before you make any instead.
Pass it back to your D and regroup since you're not sure what to do with the extra space given.
Carry the puck in since the defender is backing off and giving you time and space to attack.
Dump it in deep because that's what the forecheck system calls for regardless of what.
U15 / Bantam.why: When a defender backs off and gives you space, carrying the puck in is the right read. Dumping when you have space wastes a possession advantage.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Defender giving you space? Carry it in. Don't dump when you have time.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck carrier dumps the puck in and skates past you along the boards. They no longer have the puck. Should you finish the check?
opts: No — save your energy for the next play and let them go past you.
Yes — hit every player near you regardless of where the puck went.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
Yes — finishing the check discourages them from coming back for it.
U15 / Bantam.why: Finishing the check on a player who just released the puck is legal and smart. It separates them from the play.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Just dumped it? Finish the check. Legal and effective.

U15 / Bantam.concept: Puck protection below the goal line
U15 / Bantam.sit: You have the puck below the goal line in the offensive zone. A defender is closing on you. You're not in a position to pass. What do you do?
opts: Use your body to shield the puck and buy time until a teammate gets open for a pass option.
Skate behind the net at full speed to the other side since speed alone will beat.
Try to force a centering pass through the defender's legs since that might create a chance.
Leave the puck against the boards and skate away since there's no play to be made.
U15 / Bantam.why: Below the goal line, puck protection is king. Use your body as a shield, keep the puck on your backhand, and buy time for support to arrive.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Below the goal line with pressure? Shield the puck. Buy time. Wait for support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In the defensive zone, your winger's check cuts across the ice toward your side. Your check stays put. Should you and your winger switch assignments?
opts: No — always stay with your original assignment regardless of where they go on the ice during.
Yes — switch and communicate so each D-man stays in their zone and covers the nearest threat.
Only switch if the coach specifically told you to switch before the game or during the last timeout.
Let the winger decide since they had the original assignment and it's their responsibility.
U15 / Bantam.why: Switching when opponents cross is smart defensive zone play. The key is communication — both players need to know who has who.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Opponent crosses zones? Switch and communicate. Don't chase across the ice.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure in your own zone. You get the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are closing. Your only option is the glass. Do you use it?
opts: No — always try to make a play with the puck even if that means holding it under heavy pressure.
Yes — but aim for a teammate at the blue line to turn the clear into a breakout opportunity.
Yes — get the puck off the glass and out of the zone to relieve the immediate pressure safely.
No — freeze the puck and take a faceoff instead since that resets the play more effectively.
U15 / Bantam.why: Using the glass to exit the zone under heavy pressure is a smart play. It relieves pressure and gets the puck out safely.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Heavy pressure, no options? Use the glass. Get it out. Live to play another shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your D has the puck behind the net. The forecheck sends one player hard. You're the center. Where should you be?
opts: In the corner on the same side helping the D win the board battle.
Behind the net with the D so you can take a direct handoff.
At the far blue line waiting for a long stretch pass over instead.
In the middle around the hashmarks providing a short central option.
U15 / Bantam.why: The center's breakout job is to be the middle option at the hashmarks — short, safe, and transitions the puck quickly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Center on the breakout: hashmarks. Be the middle option.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're forechecking and the D is going to the boards to play the puck. You arrive at the same time. How do you win this battle?
opts: Arrive with speed and pin them to the boards — use your body to separate them from the puck first.
Skate past them and go behind the net to wait for the puck to come around the boards to you.
Stop before the boards and wait to see what they do with the puck before engaging in any.
Slow down before you arrive and try to poke check the puck away without making any body contact.
U15 / Bantam.why: Winning the wall means arriving with speed and using your body. Pin them, separate them from the puck, and get possession.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Forechecking to the wall: arrive with speed. Pin. Separate. Win the puck.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D making a breakout pass. Your winger is open on the wall but skating at full speed. Where do you put the pass?
opts: Behind them so they have to turn around and come back for the puck.
Tape-to-tape — lead them slightly so the puck arrives in stride without them slowing down at all.
Loft it high in the air so it goes over the forechecker's stick and lands near your winger up ahead.
Dump it in and change immediately instead of pushing the transition.
U15 / Bantam.why: A breakout pass to a player in stride must lead them. Tape-to-tape in stride keeps the speed of the transition — a pass to their feet kills the momentum.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Breakout pass to a player in stride: lead them. Tape-to-tape. Keep the speed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team turns the puck over in the offensive zone. You're the high forward. The opposing center has the puck. What's your job?
opts: Chase the puck carrier from behind and try to poke check.
Stay in the offensive zone in case your team gets the puck back.
Pick up the most dangerous opposing player and skate with them back.
Skate to your bench for a change since fresh legs are more instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: The high forward's backchecking job is to pick up the most dangerous player. Chasing the puck carrier from behind rarely works.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Turnover? Pick up a check. Don't chase — cover the dangerous player.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Their best player is coming at you 1-on-1. They're known for their speed and deking ability. They've beaten you before with a wide fake and cut back inside. How do you approach?
opts: Back up to the goal line and give them space since they're too skilled to challenge one-on-one at any gap in the neutral.
Aggressively close your gap and try to force a turnover at the blue line before they can get up to speed and start their moves.
Stand completely still at the top of the circles and wait for them to make the first move before reacting to anything they do.
Take away the inside lane and force them to the outside — accept the lower-percentage shot from the angle over a deke from slot.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a skilled deking player, taking away the inside lane and forcing them wide reduces their options. They have to beat you from a bad angle instead of the slot.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Skilled deker? Take away inside. Force them wide. Bad angle beats slot deke.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone with full possession. Both wingers are below the dots cycling. Both D are at the blue line. The opposing team has dropped into a passive box. Should one D jump into the play?
opts: Yes — one D steps down to create a numerical advantage low while the other holds the line as safety in case of a turnover or breakout.
No — both D should always stay at the blue line since leaving the line is too risky regardless of how much possession your team has.
Yes — both D should jump into the play since you have full possession and there's no risk of a breakout when your team controls.
No — the D should pass the puck between each other at the blue line until a shooting lane opens up and then take a long-range.
U15 / Bantam.why: With full possession against a passive box, one D jumping down creates an overload. The key is one stays back as safety. Both going creates transition risk.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Passive box? One D steps down for the overload. Other holds the line.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team just lost a tough game. The room is quiet. Some players are visibly upset. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Give a long speech about everything that went wrong so the team knows exactly what to fix before the next game and doesn't repeat the same.
Acknowledge the tough loss, point out something specific the team did well, and remind them of the next opportunity to compete and improve as a group.
Stay silent and let the coach handle everything since it's not your job to say anything after a loss the coaching staff's responsibility to.
Blame specific players for the loss so the team knows who was responsible and can hold them accountable for the mistakes that cost the game that instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: Post-loss leadership is about honesty and perspective. Acknowledging the loss, finding a positive, and looking forward gives the team something to build on.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tough loss? Acknowledge it. Find one positive. Point to the next one. That's leadership.

U18 / Midget.sit: An attacker receives a pass in the slot and is coming at you with a deke move. They're known for a forehand-to-backhand roof deke. You're at the top of the crease. What's your approach?
opts: Back up deeper into the net to give yourself more reaction time since the closer you are to the goal line the more time you have to react to their deke move and make the save on.
Poke check aggressively since taking the puck off their stick before they can start the deke is the only reliable way to stop a skilled deker in the slot at the U18 level.
Stay patient on your feet as long as possible — respect their move but don't bail early. When they commit to the forehand, track the puck and push across if they go backhand side.
Drop to butterfly immediately since most slot dekes require the puck to go low at some point and being in butterfly covers the bottom of the net where the majority of deke goals.
U18 / Midget.why: Patience on your feet against a slot deke forces the attacker to commit first. Bailing into butterfly early gives them the top of the net. Stay up, track the puck, then react.
U18 / Midget.tip: Slot deke? Stay on your feet. Be patient. Let them commit. Then react.

U18 / Midget.concept: Reading the shooter on a breakaway
U18 / Midget.sit: A breakaway is coming. The shooter is approaching at moderate speed — they're not rushing. They're a known deker. At what point do you commit to your save?
opts: Poke check from the top of the circles since stopping the breakaway before they get a shot is always better than trying to make a save and the poke check eliminates.
Commit early and aggressively since the best way to stop a deker is to take away as much net as possible before they start their move and force them to go around you.
Drop to butterfly immediately when they cross the blue line since most breakaway goals go low and covering the bottom of the net early gives you the best chance against.
Wait as long as possible — let them show their hand first, then react. Committing early against a deker gives them the advantage since they can adjust to your commitment easily.
U18 / Midget.why: Against a known deker, patience is your weapon. Let them show their move, then react. Committing early gives them the read and the advantage.
U18 / Midget.tip: Known deker? Be patient. Let them show you. Then commit. Patience wins.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your D has the puck in the corner and the forecheck is closing. You're the winger. Where do you need to be?
opts: Up the wall on your side — be the outlet and give the D a clear, safe passing option up the ice.
Behind the D to provide close physical support in case they need help winning the board.
In the slot area in front of the net since that's always the most dangerous area to receive.
At the far blue line waiting for a stretch pass since that creates the biggest transition.
U18 / Midget.why: The winger's wall position is the primary breakout outlet. Getting up the wall fast and being ready for the pass is your most important job on the breakout.
U18 / Midget.tip: D in the corner? Get up the wall. Be the outlet. Fast.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team leads by one with five minutes left. The other team is pressing. What's the team-wide approach?
opts: Play the exact same way you've played all game since changing your approach shows you're scared.
Smart clears, no turnovers in the neutral zone, and take what the defense gives you without risk.
Get ultra-aggressive and try to score another goal to put the game away with an insurance tally.
Turtle completely — dump the puck out every time you touch it and don't try to create any.
U18 / Midget.why: Protecting a lead late means smart hockey — clean exits, no neutral zone turnovers, and taking what's given without unnecessary risk. It's not turtling, it's managing.
U18 / Midget.tip: Lead with five left? Smart clears. No turnovers. Take what they give you.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your D is behind the net with the puck. The forecheck has overloaded the strong side — both forecheckers went to the same side. What breakout option does this create?
opts: Hold the puck behind the net and wait for the forecheck to back off before making any breakout.
Pass up the strong side anyway since that's the standard breakout and you should stick to.
Reverse the puck to the weak side where the ice is wide open since the forecheck left it vacant.
Shoot the puck off the glass on the strong side to get it past the forecheckers and out.
U18 / Midget.why: An overloaded forecheck leaves the weak side open. Reversing the puck behind the net exploits their aggressiveness and creates a clean exit.
U18 / Midget.tip: Forecheck overloaded one side? Reverse. The weak side is open.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're entering the offensive zone with possession. The D is backing up and giving you the blue line. What type of entry should you make?
opts: Pass the puck backward to your D and let them carry it in since D entries are more effective.
Dump it in hard to the corner since every zone entry should be a dump to protect against turnovers.
Stop at the blue line and wait for your entire team to catch up before you enter the zone.
Carry the puck in with speed and possession since the D is conceding the blue line to you.
U18 / Midget.why: When the D gives you the blue line, carry the puck in. A possession entry against a retreating D creates more offense than a dump-in.
U18 / Midget.tip: D backing up? Carry it in. Possession entry. Don't give up the puck for free.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is down by one goal entering the third period. The other team plays a tight defensive system. What's the overall approach?
opts: Increase offensive zone time through sustained pressure and cycling — force turnovers in their zone to create chances rather than rushing the attack up the ice.
Pull the goalie immediately at the start of the third to get an extra attacker for the full twenty minutes since you need to score and this maximizes your.
Keep playing the same way a lucky bounce since changing your approach when you're down usually makes things worse and creates more turnovers.
Play ultra-aggressive with no regard for defense since you need a goal and the best defense when trailing is a good offense that puts constant pressure on.
U18 / Midget.why: Trailing by one requires sustained offensive zone pressure, not desperation. Cycling, winning puck battles, and creating chances through possession is more effective than rush attacks against a tight system.
U18 / Midget.tip: Down one in the third? Sustained pressure. Cycle. Win battles. Don't force it.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your coach wants to run a 1-3-1 trap in the neutral zone to slow down a fast team. You're the center in the middle of the three. What's your specific role?
opts: Chase the puck carrier wherever they go since the center in the 1-3-1 should always be closest to the puck regardless of where it is on the ice at all.
Control the middle of the ice — take away the center lane and force everything wide where your D can close on the puck carrier as they approach your blue line.
Stand at center ice and don't move since the 1-3-1 is a passive system where everyone stays in their spot and waits for the opponent to make a mistake first.
Drop back to the blue line and play as a third defenseman since the neutral zone isn't where the trap works and the real defense starts at your own blue line.
U18 / Midget.why: The center in a 1-3-1 controls the middle lane. You're the key to the trap — take away the center and funnel everything wide where the structure has advantages.
U18 / Midget.tip: Center in the 1-3-1: own the middle lane. Force everything wide.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing team is trying to carry the puck through the neutral zone. Your team is in a 1-2-2. You're one of the two forwards at the red line. What's your job?
opts: Back up to your own blue line and let them enter the zone since the neutral zone doesn't instead.
Chase the puck carrier aggressively regardless of where they are since speed solves every.
Clog the middle of the ice and force the puck carrier to the outside where they have less options.
Move to the same side as the other forward at the red line so you can double-team the puck.
U18 / Midget.why: The two forwards in a 1-2-2 clog the middle. Forcing the carrier outside reduces their options — wide entries are lower percentage than middle-lane attacks.
U18 / Midget.tip: 1-2-2 middle forward: clog the center. Force them wide.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is in a tight game. The coach calls a timeout after a goal against. You have 30 seconds. What's the most productive way to use this time as a player?
opts: Drink water and zone out since the timeout is for the coach to talk and you just need physical rest not tactical information during these short breaks in the game flow.
Use the time to argue about the goal that was just scored and assign blame for the breakdown since accountability during timeouts prevents the same mistake from happening.
Talk over the coach to share what you've been seeing on the ice since your perspective is more important than theirs and the team needs to hear your observations.
Listen to the coach, catch your breath, and focus on one specific adjustment for the next shift to execute — don't try to process everything at once or change too many things.
U18 / Midget.why: Timeout productivity: listen, breathe, focus on one adjustment. Trying to fix everything at once during a 30-second break creates confusion. One focus, one fix.
U18 / Midget.tip: Timeout: listen, breathe, one focus. Don't overload. One adjustment at a time.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is down by one with 90 seconds left. You have a faceoff in the offensive zone. The coach signals to pull the goalie for the extra attacker. What's the team's approach?
opts: Set up the 6-on-5 with the extra player at the net front or point — use the overload to create a shot through traffic or a cross-ice play with the numerical advantage.
Panic and shoot the puck every time you touch it since you need a goal and shot volume is the only thing that matters when the goalie is pulled and time is.
Don't pull the goalie since an empty-net goal against ends the game and the risk outweighs the reward of having six attackers when there's still 90 seconds left to.
Have the extra player roam around the zone without a specific assignment since having an extra body creates natural chaos that the defending team can not handle.
U18 / Midget.why: 6-on-5 requires structure. The extra player takes a specific position — net front or point — to create the overload. Random movement wastes the advantage.
U18 / Midget.tip: 6-on-5: extra player takes a specific spot. Net front or point. Structure wins.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're F1 on the backcheck. Your team just turned the puck over. The opposing D has the puck and their center is open in the middle. What's your assignment?
opts: Go to the bench for a change since backchecking F1 should be a fresh player and you've been on the ice too long to be effective in a footrace with the opposing center.
Take the center away — get in the middle lane and prevent the pass to the most dangerous player in their breakout since the center pass starts their transition cleanly.
Chase the D who has the puck since taking the puck away directly is always better than covering a passing lane and your speed should be enough to catch them from here.
Skate to the far winger's side since they're the most open player on the ice and a long pass to the winger creates a bigger offensive threat than the center in the middle.
U18 / Midget.why: F1 backcheck takes away the center. The middle lane is the most dangerous breakout pass — eliminating it forces the D to use the boards, which is slower and easier to defend.
U18 / Midget.tip: F1 backcheck: take the center away. Eliminate the middle lane.

U18 / Midget.sit: In the defensive zone, the opposing team passes the puck from the corner to the point. Your winger was covering the point but got caught low. You're the center. What do you do?
opts: Don't rotate and let the point player have the shot since shots from the point are low percentage and your goalie can handle a point shot without any defensive help from you.
Stay in the slot since that's always the center's responsibility and if the winger got caught low that's their mistake to fix and not your responsibility to cover for them.
Rotate up to cover the point player since your winger can't get there — communicate so the coverage gap is filled before the point player can shoot or pass it down to the slot.
Follow the puck to the corner since the puck is what scores goals and being near the puck is always the right decision regardless of where your coverage assignment is.
U18 / Midget.why: Defensive coverage is fluid. When a winger gets caught, the center rotates to fill the gap. Communication prevents confusion. Rigid assignments without rotation create open shooters.
U18 / Midget.tip: Winger caught low? Rotate to the point. Fill the gap. Communicate.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has a PP offensive zone faceoff with a set play designed: draw back to the point for a one-timer. The PK knows this play — they've seen it. What adjustment do you make to the play?
opts: Abandon the set play entirely and just try to win the draw forward aggressively for a quick shot since set plays don't work when the other team knows what you're instead.
Run the exact same play without any adjustment since execution beats scouting and if you win the draw cleanly the PK can't stop a clean point shot regardless of what.
Skip the faceoff entirely — don't even try to win it and focus all your energy on defending the PK's counter-attack since their scouting has made your PP faceoff.
Run the same draw but add a secondary option — if the PK overplays the point shot, have the center fake the draw-back and go directly to the net with the puck instead.
U18 / Midget.why: Adding a secondary option to a scouted play keeps the PK guessing. If they overplay the point shot, the center going direct to the net exploits their adjustment.
U18 / Midget.tip: PK knows your play? Add a secondary option. Keep them guessing.

U18 / Midget.sit: There are 10 seconds left in the second period. Your team has the puck in the neutral zone. What's the smart play?
opts: Get the puck deep into the offensive zone and create a scoring chance — a late-period goal changes momentum going into the intermission break between periods.
Shoot the puck on net from center ice since any shot from anywhere is a good shot when time is running out and you might score a lucky goal from long distance at.
Dump the puck in and go for a change since the period is essentially over and there's no point in trying to create a scoring chance with only 10 seconds.
Hold the puck at center ice and let the clock run out since there isn't enough time to create anything meaningful and risking a turnover at the buzzer could.
U18 / Midget.why: 10 seconds is enough to get a shot. Getting the puck deep and creating a late-period chance can shift momentum. Wasting the opportunity is a missed chance.
U18 / Midget.tip: 10 seconds left with possession? Get it deep. Create a chance. Momentum matters.

U18 / Midget.sit: It's game day against the first-place team. Your team has lost to them twice this season. As a leader, how do you approach the pre-game room?
opts: Talk about how the other team isn't that good and you should have beaten them both times since overconfidence will motivate your team to play.
Bring calm confidence — acknowledge the challenge but focus on specific things your team does well that can win this game tonight against them.
Don't say anything since the coach handles pre-game talks and players should just focus on their own individual preparation without at this level.
Give an emotional speech about how important this game is and how everyone needs to play their best game of the season or the season is.
U18 / Midget.why: Pre-game leadership is about calm confidence. Acknowledge the challenge, focus on specific strengths, and set a tone of preparation, not panic.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big game? Calm confidence. Specific strengths. Set the tone.

U18 / Midget.concept: Penalty kill strategy late in game
U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is up by one with four minutes left and takes a penalty. The opposing PP is strong. What's the PK strategy?
opts: Aggressive PK — pressure the puck carrier at every opportunity since taking the puck away ends the power play instantly and that's better than sitting back and.
Send a player after the PP puck carrier every time to try to force a shorthanded chance since the best PK is one that scores a goal and that would seal the game.
Ultra-disciplined box — collapse to the net, block shots, and clear every puck. Deny the high-danger shots and force them to score from the perimeter if they can at all.
Play a normal PK without any adjustment for the game situation since your PK should be the same regardless of the score or the time remaining on the clock in the game.
U18 / Midget.why: Late-game PK with a lead: ultra-disciplined. Collapse, block, clear. The goal is to survive the two minutes — not create offense. Force them to score from low-percentage areas.
U18 / Midget.tip: Leading late, PK? Ultra-disciplined. Collapse. Block. Clear. Survive the two minutes.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is in 3-on-3 overtime. You have possession. There's a ton of open ice. What's the strategic approach?
opts: Pass the puck between your three players at center ice without attacking since the other team will eventually make a mistake and give you a perfect 2-on-0 or breakaway.
Dump the puck in every time and forecheck since dump-and-chase works in 3-on-3 just like it works in 5-on-5 and it's the safest way to generate offensive zone time in after that.
Attack immediately every time you get the puck since 3-on-3 is about scoring and hesitation gives the defense time to set up and take away the open ice that overtime creates.
Control the puck and wait for the right moment to attack — don't force anything since a turnover in 3-on-3 creates a high-danger chance the other way with open ice everywhere.
U18 / Midget.why: 3-on-3 is about controlled aggression. Control the puck, read the play, and attack when the opening appears. Forcing the play creates turnovers — and turnovers in OT are breakaways.
U18 / Midget.tip: 3-on-3 OT: controlled aggression. Wait for the moment. Don't force it.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing coach calls a timeout. When play resumes, you notice they've changed their forecheck from a 1-2-2 to a more aggressive 2-1-2. What do you do with this recognition?
opts: Adjust your breakout immediately — use the weak side and quick D-to-D passes since a 2-1-2 overloads one side and leaves the other side more open for a clean breakout exit.
Ignore it since trying to identify and adjust to mid-game tactical changes is overthinking and you should just run your standard breakout regardless of what they're.
Panic and call your own timeout since the opponent has changed tactics and your team needs time to discuss and plan a completely new strategy to counter their aggressive.
Slow the game down and hold the puck in your own zone to waste time and prevent the new forecheck from being effective since if you don't have the puck they can't.
U18 / Midget.why: Recognizing a mid-game tactical change and adjusting immediately is elite hockey IQ. A 2-1-2 overloads one side — the weak side is the outlet.
U18 / Midget.tip: They changed their forecheck after a timeout? Recognize it. Adjust. Use the weak side.

U18 / Midget.concept: NZ faceoff with game on the line
U18 / Midget.sit: Neutral zone faceoff with 10 seconds left. Game is tied. Your coach wants you to win the draw and get a shot. The opposing center is one of the best faceoff players in the league. What's your approach?
opts: Cheat on the draw and try to get the puck before it hits the ice since the referee probably won't kick you out with 10 seconds left and the advantage of getting the puck first.
Study their stance and hand position — adjust your technique to counter their strength. Have your wingers ready to jump on a loose puck if the draw isn't won cleanly by either side.
Let them win the draw and focus on defense since with 10 seconds left it's better to play for overtime than to risk a turnover that gives them a chance to win it in regulation.
Take the draw the same way you always do since adjusting your technique at the last second is more likely to cause you to lose the draw than to win it against a strong faceoff.
U18 / Midget.why: Big faceoffs require preparation and support. Study the opposing center, counter their technique, and have your wingers ready for a loose puck. It's not just the center's battle.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big faceoff: study them. Counter their technique. Wingers ready for the loose puck.

U18 / Midget.sit: It's the championship game. Pre-game nerves are high. Some teammates look tight. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Tell everyone to pretend it's a regular game since acknowledging the significance of the championship will add to the pressure and the team should treat it like any.
Give an intense, emotional speech about how this is the most important game of everyone's life and anyone who doesn't perform at their best will regret it for the rest.
Keep the room loose but focused — remind the team to play their game, trust the preparation, and enjoy the moment since this is what you've worked for the entire season.
Don't say anything since every player handles pressure differently and any pre-game talk might make the nervous players feel worse and the confident players feel.
U18 / Midget.why: Championship leadership: loose but focused. Trust the preparation, play your game, enjoy the moment. Over-intensity creates tightness. Dismissing the moment loses its power.
U18 / Midget.tip: Championship game? Loose but focused. Trust preparation. Enjoy the moment.

U18 / Midget.concept: Managing emotions after a bad hit
U18 / Midget.sit: An opponent delivers a questionable hit on your linemate. No penalty is called. Your linemate is shaken up. The bench is furious. What do you do on the next shift?
opts: Go after the player who delivered the hit and fight them since your teammate needs to know you have their back and physical retaliation is the only language some players.
Take a run at the nearest opposing player to send a message to the other team that they can't get away with hits like that without consequences from your team in the next shift.
Ignore it completely and play passively since getting emotional after a bad hit is exactly what the other team wants and the best response is to pretend nothing happened at all.
Play hard and physical but within the rules — channel the emotion into compete level, not retaliation, since a penalty hurts your team more than it helps your linemate.
U18 / Midget.why: Channeling emotion into compete, not retaliation, is elite emotional management. A penalty after a bad hit is exactly what the other team wants. Play harder, not dirtier.
U18 / Midget.tip: Bad hit on a teammate? Channel the emotion into compete. Don't give them the penalty.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your PK just killed a big penalty and your bench is energized. You're the first player on the ice after the penalty expires. What do you do with the momentum?
opts: Channel the energy into a strong, aggressive shift — the PK momentum carry-over often leads to scoring chances if the team capitalizes on the emotional shift immediately.
Ignore the momentum since hockey is played in individual shifts and what happened on the PK has no bearing on what happens in the next five-on-five shift for your.
Calm down and play conservatively since the PK was tiring and the team needs to recover their energy before pushing the pace again so you should ease into the next.
Call a timeout to celebrate the kill and let the momentum sink in since stopping play after a big PK allows the team to savor the moment and build even more emotional.
U18 / Midget.why: PK momentum is real. The first shift after a big kill is often the best chance to capitalize — the team is energized and the opponent is frustrated their PP failed.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big PK? First shift after: channel the energy. Momentum is real. Capitalize.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're on the PK and the PP is cycling. The puck goes below the goal line to the player behind the net. Your PK has been passive. Should you pressure behind the net?
opts: No — the PK should never pressure above or below the goal line since the box formation is designed to work without any players leaving their assigned positions at any point.
Yes — controlled pressure behind the net disrupts the cycle setup and can force a turnover before the PP can get organized in the dangerous areas of the offensive zone above the dots.
No — stay in the box and let the goalie handle anything from behind the net since leaving the box creates gaps that the PP will exploit with quick passes to open players in the slot.
Yes — send two PK players behind the net aggressively to trap the puck carrier and force a turnover since a shorthanded 2-on-1 behind the net will overwhelm the PP puck carrier.
U18 / Midget.why: Controlled pressure behind the net disrupts the cycle. The key word is controlled — one player pressures, the box adjusts. You're not trapping, you're disrupting.
U18 / Midget.tip: Puck behind the net on PP? Controlled pressure. Disrupt, don't trap.

U18 / Midget.concept: NZ play with the lead in the third
U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is up by one in the third. The other team is pressing. Your neutral zone play has been loose — they're getting clean entries. What do you tighten up?
opts: Open up the NZ and let them enter since trying to prevent entries is futile when a team is pressing and you should focus all your defensive energy on the zone defense after.
Have all five players back behind the blue line before the opposing team reaches the neutral zone so there's no chance of a clean entry through the NZ since depth is more.
Clog the middle lane, backcheck harder to the puck side, and force everything wide — clean entries are coming through the middle so take it away systematically with your positioning.
Play the same NZ system since you're winning and changing anything in the third period shows the other team you're afraid and gives them confidence that their pressure is.
U18 / Midget.why: Clean entries through the middle mean the NZ is too loose. Clogging the middle and forcing wide entries reduces the quality of their zone possessions.
U18 / Midget.tip: Clean entries through the middle? Clog it. Force wide. Reduce quality.

U18 / Midget.sit: The season is over. Your team didn't make it as far as you hoped. As a leader, what's the most important message in the final team meeting?
opts: Don't say anything emotional since the season is over and there's nothing anyone can do about it now — everyone should just go home and move on to whatever comes next in their lives.
Focus entirely on what went wrong so the team understands their failures and doesn't repeat the same mistakes next year since accountability after a disappointing season is.
Blame the coaching staff for the team's failures since the players did their best and the results were a reflection of poor coaching decisions and strategy throughout the.
Acknowledge the disappointment, celebrate what the team accomplished and grew through, and challenge everyone to use the offseason to come back better for next year together.
U18 / Midget.why: End-of-season leadership: acknowledge, celebrate, challenge. The balance between processing disappointment and finding forward momentum defines how a team handles adversity.
U18 / Midget.tip: Season over, fell short? Acknowledge, celebrate, challenge. That's how leaders close a season.

U18 / Midget.concept: D reading the forecheck in real time
U18 / Midget.sit: You're a D retrieving a dump-in. You scan: F1 closing hard from the right, F2 middle, F3 high in the NZ. Your winger is on the left wall. What do you do?
opts: Quick pass to the left winger on the wall — F1 is coming from the right so the left side is the safe outlet. The scan told you everything you needed in one second to make the play.
Carry the puck up the right side directly at F1 since the best way to beat a forecheck is to attack it head-on and your puck skills should be good enough to beat one in this case.
Hold the puck and wait to see what happens since making decisions in one second is too fast and you need more time to process all the information before committing to.
Shoot the puck off the glass and out of the zone since any time you're under forecheck pressure the safest play is to clear the zone and let the team reset on the next possession.
U18 / Midget.why: One-second scans dictate breakout decisions. F1 from the right means the left is open. Quick processing and execution is the difference between a clean breakout and a turnover.
U18 / Midget.tip: Scan: one second. F1 right = outlet left. Quick process, quick execute.
